summary: Big day in Reynoldstown as that long-stalled BeltLine development finally breaks ground after sitting empty for five years. Meanwhile, Atlanta's airport turned into a circus with ICE agents deployed to help TSA lines that stretched for hours.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown BeltLine Development Finally Gets Moving After Five Years
topStory.summary: That corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting empty since 2021 finally has construction crews working. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving ahead with 85 residential units just a block from the BeltLine's Eastside Trail. About time something happened there.
topStory.body:
If you've walked the BeltLine through Reynoldstown lately, you've definitely noticed that cleared lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting there like a missing tooth since 2021. Well, good news: construction crews are finally back, and this thing is actually happening.

Embry Development Company picked up the project from Toll Brothers two years ago and is moving ahead with what they're calling Camber Crossing. We're talking 85 residential units (mix of townhomes and condos) about a block west of the Eastside Trail, right where all the BeltLine action is. The original permits were already in place, so Embry just had to get organized and get to work.

This is exactly the kind of infill development Reynoldstown needs right now. The neighborhood's been watching property values climb as the BeltLine brings more foot traffic and amenities, but it still needs more housing options for people who actually want to live near all that. Whether these units will be affordable enough for regular folks remains to be seen, but at least something's finally happening on that corner instead of just weeds and construction fencing.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at Reynoldstown BeltLine development

Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving ahead with 85 residential units just a block from the BeltLine's Eastside Trail. About time something happened there.

stories.imageAlt: Construction site at Reynoldstown BeltLine development

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 18 'Missing Middle' Cottages for Under $700K
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway on Midway Road is trying to crack the affordability problem with 18 standalone houses around shared courtyards. With Decatur home prices hitting $700K+, these cottage-style homes are designed to give people an ITP option that doesn't require a tech salary.
stories.body:
You know how impossible it's gotten to buy anything ITP for less than your firstborn child? Well, Cottages at Midway thinks they've found a workaround. This village-style development on Midway Road in unincorporated Decatur is putting 18 standalone houses around shared courtyards and green space, aiming for that sweet spot between apartments and McMansions.

The timing couldn't be better. Decatur's median home price just crossed $700,000, which is absolutely wild for a place that was still considered 'transitional' not that long ago. The developers are calling this 'missing middle' housing, and they're right. There's basically nothing between $400K condos and $800K+ houses right now.

Construction's already underway on the 2.1-acre site, and they're doing a soft launch next week to start drumming up interest. Whether they can actually deliver on the affordability promise remains to be seen, but at least someone's trying to build housing that normal humans can afford near a major commercial corridor and close to actual Decatur.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Finally Taking Shape
stories.summary: The GW Parkside development at 600 Hopkins Street is starting to look like actual buildings after demolition began a year ago. Ackerman & Co. (the Lee + White folks) are bringing 23 townhomes to market this spring, with the first seven units almost ready.
stories.body:
Remember that demo work happening last year at Hopkins Street and White Street in the West End? Well, those townhomes are finally starting to look like something. Ackerman & Co., the same crew behind Lee + White's food and office scene, has been working on GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) and the first phase is almost ready for buyers.

This is pretty significant for the West End stretch of the BeltLine, which has seen way less development action than the eastside corridor. You've got 23 for-sale townhomes coming to market this spring, starting with seven units that are basically topped out. For-sale housing near the BeltLine that isn't crazy expensive? That's getting rarer by the minute.

Ackerman knows what they're doing here. They've already proven with Lee + White that they can create something that feels authentic to the neighborhood while still drawing people from across the city. If they can pull off the same balance with residential development, this could be exactly what the West End needs to keep longtime residents from getting priced out while still growing the community.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect Peking chicken platters, soft shell crab bao, and Thai tea martinis in a space that started as a pop-up and finally has a permanent home.
stories.body:
After years of pop-ups and working in other people's kitchens, chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own place. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, right where you can easily hop off the Freedom Park Trail for lunch or dinner.

The menu is exactly what you'd want from these two: Asian-American comfort food that actually makes sense. Their Peking chicken feeds two people for $25, which is practically unheard of these days. You've also got soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab rangoon puffs, and cocktails that don't take themselves too seriously. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, so she knows her way around a kitchen.

What's great about this spot is the location. You're right off one of the best trail connections in the city, in a neighborhood that's managed to keep some of its character despite all the development pressure. Le and Jimenez have been testing their concept for a while now, so they know what works. This feels like the kind of place Inman Park needed - good food, reasonable prices, and actual neighborhood vibes instead of trying to be the next big Instagram destination.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The guy who created NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing Vela Park Food Works to the East Lake area in mid-April. Six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining at the new rental community near East Lake Golf Club.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows food halls. The guy created Gansevoort Market in New York, and now he's bringing his third Atlanta project to the eastside. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction at the new Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club, with plans to open in mid-April.

This isn't just another food court situation. We're talking six different chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and roomy outdoor dining at the nearly 12-acre rental complex. Montwaid's track record in Atlanta has been solid, so he clearly knows how to make these multi-vendor concepts work in our market.

The East Lake area has been waiting for something like this. You've got all these new residents moving into the area, but the dining options haven't really kept pace with the development. A well-executed food hall with outdoor space and quality vendors could be exactly what that part of town needs to feel more complete as a neighborhood instead of just a place where people sleep between commutes downtown.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style Brisket to the BeltLine
stories.summary: John Lewis opened his smokehouse at Ansley Mall with six 22-foot smokers running 24/7 in a loft overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. This is serious Texas-style barbecue with a view of the trail action.
stories.body:
The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue looks like something out of a barbecue fever dream. Six 22-foot smokers fill a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. They run 24 hours a day in eight-hour shifts, which is exactly the kind of obsessive approach you want from your pitmaster.

John Lewis helped weld those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and he's putting out some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta. This is Austin-by-way-of-Charleston barbecue, which means it's serious about the craft without being pretentious about it. The location at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse puts you right in the heart of BeltLine foot traffic.

What's brilliant about this setup is how it combines serious barbecue with the BeltLine experience. You can grab food and eat while watching people walk, run, and bike past on the trail. It's exactly the kind of authentic but accessible addition the BeltLine needs more of, instead of just another trendy spot that forgets food is supposed to be good before it's Instagrammable.

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Project Nearly Done
stories.summary: The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle now has sidewalks, a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, and traffic calming measures. After nearly a decade in planning, Decatur's push to make West Howard more people-friendly is almost finished.
stories.body:
Decatur has been working on transforming West Howard Avenue from a high-speed car route into something more civilized for nearly a decade, and it's finally almost done. The roughly one-mile stretch between Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) and the city's western limits at Paden Circle now has the full Complete Streets treatment.

We're talking sidewalks, infrastructure upgrades, repaving, and most importantly, a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers for most of the route. This isn't just painted bike lanes that cars ignore - it's actual protected space for people on bikes. The project also includes traffic calming measures that should make the whole corridor feel less like a drag strip.

This is exactly the kind of infrastructure investment that makes a real difference in how people move around the city. West Howard connects downtown Decatur to the northern edge of East Lake MARTA station, so it's a key route for people trying to get around without a car. The fact that Decatur actually followed through on the complete streets vision instead of just talking about it puts them ahead of most metro Atlanta cities.
stories.imageAlt: Two-way cycle track on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy to Atlanta Airport as TSA Lines Stretch for Hours
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are walking around Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security as the government shutdown drags on and TSA workers call out sick. But travelers say the agents aren't actually doing much besides standing around while lines still take four hours.
stories.body:
The world's busiest airport has turned into a bit of a circus. Federal immigration agents have been deployed to Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security as TSA workers continue calling out sick during the partial government shutdown. But if you ask travelers, those ICE agents are mostly just standing around while people still wait four hours to get through security.

Mayor Dickens assured everyone the agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, they're just there to help with crowd control. The problem is that doesn't really solve the fundamental issue: TSA agents haven't been paid in over a month, so they're either not showing up or they're working without pay. You can't fix that with more federal agents walking around.

This is what happens when the federal government uses airports as political footballs. Hartsfield-Jackson handles more passengers than any other airport in the world, so when TSA operations fall apart here, it affects travel everywhere. Delta has even suspended special services for Congress members, which honestly seems like the least they could do when regular travelers are camping out in security lines overnight.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Detention Ban and Police Documentation Rules
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in city limits and requiring Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during operations. Full council votes April 20, while dozens showed up to criticize Dickens for not signing the firefighter union contract.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is moving forward with two resolutions aimed at limiting ICE operations in the city, even as federal immigration agents are literally walking around the airport right now. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced measures that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require APD to document whenever ICE agents are present during police operations.

The timing is pretty remarkable, considering ICE agents are currently deployed at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with TSA lines during the government shutdown. But this is about longer-term policy, not the current airport situation. The full council will vote on both resolutions April 20.

What was really interesting about the meeting was how many people showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighter union contract that council ratified last year. This was the first council meeting since that controversy blew up, and firefighters made their feelings known. The ICE resolutions got unanimous support, but the firefighter contract fight is clearly not going away anytime soon.

stories.headline: Park Pride Tackles Climate Change at 25th Annual Conference
stories.summary: Hundreds of attendees from across the Southeast packed the Atlanta Botanical Gardens for the Parks & Greenspace Conference focused on 'Resilient Parks, Resilient Communities'. The big question: how do you build resilience when maybe no city is actually a climate refuge?
stories.body:
The 25th annual Parks & Greenspace Conference drew hundreds of people to the Atlanta Botanical Gardens to wrestle with a sobering reality: climate change is coming for all of us, and parks might be our best defense. This year's theme was 'Resilient Parks, Resilient Communities,' but Park Pride Education Director Eli Dickerson opened with a stark assessment: 'Maybe no city is a climate refuge.'

That's the challenge facing Atlanta and cities across the Southeast. We used to think of ourselves as relatively protected from the worst climate impacts, but the last few years have made it clear that nowhere is really safe. The conference brought together park advocates, city planners, and community leaders to figure out how green spaces can help communities adapt and survive.

What's encouraging is how many people are taking this seriously now. Atlanta calls itself 'the city in a forest,' but that canopy is under constant pressure from development and extreme weather. The conference focused on practical solutions: how to design parks that can handle flooding, extreme heat, and severe storms while still serving communities that need them most. It's the kind of long-term thinking Atlanta needs more of.
stories.imageAlt: Parks and Greenspace Conference at Atlanta Botanical Gardens
